w:r.w:t: This document defines the visual language for Tidemarq — a self-hosted directory sync tool. It covers brand colours, typography, UX component states, chart usage, and naming conventions. All product design work should reference this guide to ensure consistency across the application, documentation, and marketing materials.

This guide is intended for use in Cowork design threads and shared with anyone contributing to the Tidemarq UI. When in doubt, refer back to this document before introducing new colours, type styles, or component patterns.
The product name is written in lowercase: tidemarq. The Q at the end is deliberate — do not capitalise or alter the spelling.
Use sentence case only. Never title case or all caps.

The logo mark is a square tile containing four horizontal lines of decreasing length, stacked bottom to top. This represents tide marks receding — the visual metaphor for tracking and holding a position. The lines always anchor on the left edge. The top line is the shortest and most transparent; the bottom line is full width and full opacity.
The primary palette runs from deep harbour (#062C36) to pale seafoam (#E0F4F7). All UI surfaces, navigation, and primary interactive elements draw from this ramp.
Page backgrounds, dark UI surfaces, footer
Sidebar, nav rail, primary text on dark
Primary brand colour — icons, links, active states
Dark mode icon background, hover highlights
Tagline colour on dark, secondary links
Light backgrounds, table row alternates, input fill
These five colours are used for data visualisation and semantic UI states. They are chosen to be visually distinct from the teal core while remaining cohesive with the overall palette.
Warnings, pending states, highlights in charts
Secondary data series in charts, badges
Use these for text, borders, and UI chrome. Do not substitute brand teal for these roles.
The product uses a single typeface family throughout: Arial. It is universally available and renders cleanly at all UI sizes without requiring a web font.
File paths and directory names should always render in a monospace font (Courier New or system-mono) at 13px. This makes paths scannable and distinct from descriptive text.
Every sync job and directory entry sits in one of four states at any given time. These states map consistently to colours, icons, and label text across the entire application.
Both source and target are in agreement. No action needed.
A sync is queued or in progress. Do not interrupt.
Sync failed. User action required. Always show reason.
Excluded by user rule. Not an error, not synced intentionally.

Never use the brand teal (#0E5F6E) as a semantic state colour — it belongs to navigation and primary interactive elements only. If a new state is needed, choose from the accent palette and document it here first.
When plotting multiple data series, assign colours in this order to maximise contrast and accessibility:
Series 3: Amber (#C27D1A) — tertiary metric or threshold
Series 5: Coral (#C4432A) — negative or error metric
For single-series charts, always use mid teal. Slate (#3D5166) is reserved for neutral or background reference lines.
Always include a legend when more than one series is present
Grid lines should use the light grey border colour (#D3D1C7) at 0.5px
Use the light variant of each colour for filled areas and bar chart fills when a darker stroke is also present
Never use opacity to simulate a new colour — use the defined light variants instead
Use for comparing values across time periods or categories. Group related series side by side. Maximum four series per chart before switching to a line chart.
Use for trends over time. Minimum line weight 2px. Points optional — use only when individual values matter.
Use for showing proportion of sync states across a directory set. Maximum five segments. Always label the centre with the total file count.
Use inline with directory rows to show recent sync activity. Single colour (mid teal). No axes or labels.
Main CTA — sync now, save, confirm
Never use brand colours for disabled states
Use the semantic state colours for sync-state badges. For general categorisation tags (file type, source, etc.) use the ghost teal background (#E0F4F7) with dark teal text (#0A4452).
Input background: #E0F4F7 (ghost teal) for subtle distinction from pure white surfaces
Directories in the sync tree follow these visual rules:
Hover row: pale teal (#A3DDE6) at 30% opacity
State indicator dot: 8px circle, positioned left of the row label, semantic state colour
All spacing uses a base unit of 4px. Common values:
4px — inline gaps between icon and label
24px — between cards, between major UI sections
Sidebar width: 240px fixed. Main content area: fluid. Minimum supported viewport: 1024px wide.
Use a single consistent icon set throughout the application. Recommended: Lucide Icons (MIT licensed, 24px grid, 1.5px stroke weight).
Semantic icons follow state colour — error icons in coral, success in grass
Icon size in UI: 16px for inline, 20px for standalone, 24px for empty states
Never resize icons outside these three values
Tidemarq is a technical tool for people who care about their data. The voice should be calm, precise, and direct. It never panics, never over-explains, and never uses jargon without definition.
Use active voice: "Synced 1,240 files" not "1,240 files were synced"
Be specific about errors: name the file and the reason
Use past tense for completed actions: "Sync complete" not "Syncing complete"
Keep empty states helpful: explain why it's empty and what to do next
Don't anthropomorphise: "Tidemarq is thinking..." is off-brand
Don't abbreviate state names in the UI — spell out Synced, Pending, Error, Ignored
Don't use red for anything other than errors and destructive actions
Initial release — brand, palette, states, charts, components
Update this document whenever a new colour, component pattern, or state is introduced. Increment the minor version for additions, major version for breaking changes to existing rules.
